title: Family Dinner

jokes.text: Someone keeps eating my yogurt! / What kind of yogurt?
jokes.setup: Alexis is furious that her yogurt was stolen and yells dramatically about it.
jokes.punchline: David's response completely ignores the emotional weight of her complaint and treats it as a practical inquiry — as if the yogurt variety might change his culpability.
jokes.explanation: David's deflection is perfectly in character — his complete self-centeredness expressed through faux-innocent curiosity. The economy is excellent: three words that say everything about his character. The joke works because the audience immediately understands he ate the yogurt, and his non-answer is his answer.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, I was saving that yogurt for after my run.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm sorry. I didn't realize you were saving it for during your run instead.

jokes.text: Oh my God, I guess I was saving it for during your run, then.
jokes.setup: Alexis scolds David for eating her yogurt that she was saving for after her run.
jokes.punchline: David reframes his theft as a service — he was 'saving it' for a different time — with complete mock innocence.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed deflection. David doesn't deny eating the yogurt; he reframes the timeline with perfect passive-aggressive logic. The 'I guess' is doing heavy lifting — it's faux-resignation that's actually smug. Very specific to David's character of weaponizing politeness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, you're like a raccoon. You go through the garbage, you have those little hands, and you're always wearing the same outfit.

jokes.text: Agh, you're like a big, dirty raccoon, David!
jokes.setup: David has stolen and eaten her yogurt with no remorse.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's insult — comparing David to a raccoon rummaging through food — is specific and vivid enough to be funny.
jokes.explanation: The 'big, dirty raccoon' is a vivid and funny image — specific enough to land but not particularly elevated comedy. It's a solid character insult that feels authentically Alexis-ish. Not exceptional but functional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I can't believe we're out of food again. This is like, literally the worst thing that's ever happened to me.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're 28 years old, Alexis. And I'm standing here in a silk robe at 2 PM with no idea how to make a sandwich.

jokes.text: Oh Alexis, you're almost forty, if you want some food, prepare yourself some food!
jokes.setup: The family is complaining about food shortages in the motel.
jokes.punchline: Moira inserts a random (and inaccurate) dig at Alexis's age while making a practical point — the age number is weaponized as an aside, which is funnier than the actual advice.
jokes.explanation: Very Moira — the gratuitous age comment is delivered as incidental fact, not insult, which is funnier. It reveals her obliviousness and casual cruelty simultaneously. The 'almost forty' is almost certainly wrong, which adds an extra layer.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know how to cook.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: None of us know how to cook.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We've never had to cook.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This is literally child abuse.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, we're all adults.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Emotionally, I'm twelve.

jokes.text: Nobody can cook, this is basically child abuse!
jokes.setup: David is complaining that there is no food.
jokes.punchline: He escalates food scarcity to 'child abuse' with complete sincerity, which reveals his extreme privilege and self-dramatization.
jokes.explanation: The escalation to 'child abuse' works because it's completely earnest — David genuinely believes this. It's a solid wealth-privilege joke dressed as a complaint. The character integration is strong; this level of hyperbole is distinctly David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not using a Teppanyaki table. Do you know what happens when you put hair extensions near an open flame? It's a fire hazard. And expensive.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh please, David. I've been doing this for years without incident.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, your hair extensions melting onto my equipment and destroying it would be more than an incident. It would be a catastrophe.

jokes.text: You got that Teppanyaki table for your bar mitzvah. / That was thrown out after Alexis left her extensions on it, and everything smelled like burnt hair.
jokes.setup: Moira suggests David can cook because he got a Teppanyaki table for his bar mitzvah.
jokes.punchline: David reveals the table was ruined because Alexis left her hair extensions on it — an absurdly specific and petty piece of family history that escalates the scene's comedy.
jokes.explanation: The Teppanyaki-table-for-a-bar-mitzvah is already a great encapsulation of Rose family excess, but 'Alexis left her extensions on it and everything smelled like burnt hair' elevates it to a genuinely funny specific detail. The specificity of the hair-extension incident makes it feel like it actually happened.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You ruined the cooking equipment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was trying to make a smoothie and I may have put the blender in the dishwasher.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You put the blender in the dishwasher?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it needed to be washed.

jokes.text: I thought it was an actual table, David. / There was a plug coming out of the side of it!
jokes.setup: David reveals Alexis ruined the Teppanyaki table by leaving extensions on it.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's defense that she thought it was 'an actual table' is demolished by David's completely reasonable counter — it had a plug.
jokes.explanation: This is a tight two-line exchange that perfectly characterizes both siblings. Alexis's defense ('I thought it was a table') is already absurd, but 'There was a plug coming out of the side of it!' as a retort is beautifully exasperated and logical. The rhythm of the exchange is excellent. Strong character integration — this is David at his best: righteous and specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, it's a lamp. It requires electricity to function. That's not a personality trait, that's basic physics.

jokes.text: John struggling to say he can cook — 'Well, I-I could... I-I could cook.' / 'Oh, John, John! This is embarrassing!'
jokes.setup: The family pressures John to cook, and he's clearly out of his depth.
jokes.punchline: His halting, stammering admission ('I-I could... I-I could cook') combined with Moira's mortified reaction is the joke — an enormously successful man can't even claim basic domestic competence.
jokes.explanation: A solid character beat — John's pride vs. his complete domestic helplessness. Moira's 'This is embarrassing!' is the sharper line. The stammer is a performance note more than a writing achievement. Competent scene work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, actually, those were my recipes. I had them prepared.

jokes.text: Moira and John fondly reminiscing about 'Adelina's enchiladas' with enthusiastic 'Mm! Uh huh! Yeah! Mmm!' agreement.
jokes.setup: Moira asks what Adelina the cook used to make, and John responds 'The enchiladas.'
jokes.punchline: They both enthusiastically agree and revel in the memory — completely oblivious that they're lovingly crediting their employee for a recipe that is (allegedly) Moira's own mother's.
jokes.explanation: The payoff — that Moira will claim the recipe as her own — is set up beautifully by this oblivious duet. The 'Mm! Uh huh! Yeah!' is visual/performance comedy, but the structural irony is sharp: they're crediting someone for what is, apparently, Moira's family recipe. The humor is in their cluelessness.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not being defensive, I'm being factual.

jokes.text: Excuse me, but the enchiladas were my mother's recipe. / I don't think so. / Um, that's not how I remember it.
jokes.setup: John and Moira were just happily crediting Adelina the cook for the enchiladas.
jokes.punchline: Moira inserts her claim mid-reverie; John and Alexis immediately and casually dispute it — not cruelly, just as a matter of fact — which is funnier than outrage.
jokes.explanation: The casual dismissal from the family ('I don't think so' / 'that's not how I remember it') is funnier than any dramatic argument would be. The nonchalance wounds without trying to. Good structure: Moira's claim is interrupted almost immediately, suggesting the family's disbelief is reflexive.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You were there, Johnny. You remember. We were at that gala, and you wore that—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I... I don't think I was at a gala.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Of course you were! And you said to the ambassador—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm, well... that's... that's possible, I suppose. I mean, we've been to many... events, and I'm sure at some point I wore... something, so...

jokes.text: Yes, well, if that's the way you remember things, sweetheart, that's...
jokes.setup: Moira insists the enchilada recipe belongs to her family; John half-heartedly supports her.
jokes.punchline: John's trailing-off 'that's...' is a masterclass in diplomatic cowardice — he can't even finish the lie, which is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The unfinished sentence is beautifully written. John starts to validate Moira and simply cannot complete the thought — not because he forgets, but because he can't fully commit to a diplomatic lie. The ellipsis is the punchline. Very John Rose. Economy is nearly perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, that's so great! You're going to be cooking? That's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I didn't volunteer for this.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, I know, but still. It's going to be so fun. You're going to be amazing at it.

jokes.text: Oh my gosh David, it's gonna be so fun for you tonight, doing that with Mom. / Don't touch me! / It's so nice! / Don't! That's harassment!
jokes.setup: Moira drafts David to help her cook; Alexis finds this gleefully entertaining.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's sarcastic 'it's gonna be so fun' while presumably poking or grabbing David, who responds with escalating outrage — 'That's harassment!' is a comedically oversized reaction to sibling teasing.
jokes.explanation: 'That's harassment!' from David is the standout line — his tendency to escalate everything to legal/moral violation territory for trivial sibling contact is a signature David move. The physical interplay (inferred) amplifies it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh David, you look so handsome today.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm just going to touch your arm for a second.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Please don't.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh, come on. It's just a little touch.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's harassment. I'm calling the police. This is assault. You've crossed a line, Alexis. A very clear line.

jokes.text: Listen, a small favour... I require the use of your kitchen.
jokes.setup: Moira approaches the Schitt household with the framing of a 'small favour.'
jokes.punchline: 'I require' — the imperious phrasing reveals this is not a request but a demand dressed as a favour. The gap between 'small favour' framing and 'I require' tone is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira — she frames a bold imposition as a minor request, then uses regal language that betrays her true attitude. 'I require' is doing all the character work. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need to ask you a small favour.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Can I use your kitchen?

jokes.text: Oh really, honey? Because this pie is scorching!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn claims the stove is 'on the fritz' to avoid letting Moira use their kitchen.
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately undercuts her lie by casually noting the freshly baked, hot pie — a perfect accidental betrayal.
jokes.explanation: Tightly constructed: the lie is barely out of Jocelyn's mouth before Roland destroys it with a single observation. The economy is excellent. Roland's obliviousness (he's not trying to expose her, he's just noting the pie) makes it funnier than if he were knowingly contradicting her. A reliable comedic mechanism executed cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: May I use your kitchen?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, um, yes, of course.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Although, I'm about to use it myself.

jokes.text: It comes and goes, Roland!
jokes.setup: Roland has just blown up Jocelyn's lie about the broken stove.
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's flustered defense — 'it comes and goes!' — is clearly absurd and she knows it, which makes her desperation funny.
jokes.explanation: A good secondary beat to joke #14. Jocelyn doubling down on an obviously destroyed lie is reliably funny, but it's the weaker half of the exchange. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: The stove is broken.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's not broken. I used it this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, it wasn't working when I tried to use it.

jokes.text: Gary? Our dentist??
jokes.setup: Jocelyn desperately names people she supposedly called to fix the stove: first 'Bill,' then 'Gary.'
jokes.punchline: Roland recognizes 'Gary' as their dentist — Jocelyn is so flustered she's naming professionals who have nothing to do with stove repair.
jokes.explanation: This is a wonderfully escalating lie. 'Gary our dentist??' lands hard because of the rising specificity — Jocelyn's lying has gotten so desperate she's reached into a completely unrelated professional. Roland's genuine confusion makes it better. Very tight economy, great reaction beat.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A repairman? Which company? I know all the repairmen in town.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, um... it was... a local one.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: What was the name of the company?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I... I don't remember.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, what did he fix?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: The... the thing. You know.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, I've lived here my whole life. There's only three repairmen, and I play poker with all of them. None of them came to your house.

jokes.text: I don't remember who I called but they're coming, this afternoon!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has now named both Bill (wrong profession) and Gary the dentist as potential stove repairmen.
jokes.punchline: She abandons specificity entirely — 'I don't remember who I called' — which is the final confirmation that she's completely made this up.
jokes.explanation: The collapse of the lie into vague non-specificity is a good comedic beat, but it's the third beat in a running joke sequence. Each beat is slightly less impactful than the last here. Still funny as escalating absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I called the repair service this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, you called the dentist.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, he's very handy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: He's a dentist. You asked him to fix the stove.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: He said he'd think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Of course he did, because you called the dentist about your stove.

jokes.text: It's as if you don't trust David and me to fend for ourselves in your kitchen.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn has been making transparently weak excuses to avoid sharing the kitchen.
jokes.punchline: Moira gives her the most cutting possible interpretation — 'it's AS IF you don't trust us' — without actually admitting she knows exactly what's happening. The genteel passive-aggression is very Moira.
jokes.explanation: Moira using 'as if' to express a truth she won't state directly is a character staple. Works well but isn't a standout moment. Functional Moira characterization.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Alright Moira, you can use the kitchen.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: But you're gonna have to pay me for the electricity.

jokes.text: Oh no, we don't mind you using our kitchen, as long as you make extra for us, and leave a ten spot over there for the hydro.
jokes.setup: Moira has guilted Jocelyn into offering their kitchen.
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately pivots to transactional terms — extra food AND $10 for the electricity — revealing his mercenary practicality under the guise of hospitality.
jokes.explanation: The '$10 for the hydro' is the real punchline — petty enough to be funny, specific enough to be character-driven. Roland's casual transactionalism in social situations is a reliable comedy engine. 'Ten spot' and 'hydro' are specifically Canadian/small-town vernacular, which adds flavor.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, during the lunch rush, we get maybe two, three people coming in. And that's if Ronnie stops by for coffee. Last Tuesday, a tumbleweed actually rolled through the cafe. I'm not exaggerating. It was real.

jokes.text: That I can do! I'll be back in ten, to a clean kitchen! / A clean kitchen. / I just cleaned the kitchen!
jokes.setup: Moira agrees to Roland's terms and leaves, declaring she'll return to 'a clean kitchen.'
jokes.punchline: The three-beat exchange: Moira's imperious demand, Jocelyn's appalled echo, Roland's defensive protest — all three reactions are distinct and funny. Roland 'just cleaned it' implies Moira is ordering him to clean something he already cleaned.
jokes.explanation: Good three-part construction. Moira exits with a demand, Jocelyn absorbs the absurdity, Roland's mild protest lands the final beat. The rhythm is tight. Not exceptional but very well executed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, Twyla, I've been thinking about the state of the restaurant industry, and how it's evolved over the years. The customer experience, the ambiance, the presentation of our dishes—it all speaks to who we are as proprietors. When I opened this establishment, I had a vision, and that vision was...

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Mr. Rose, these plates are getting really hot.

jokes.text: Well, it's tasting more like meat.
jokes.setup: Twyla asks how the brisket is.
jokes.punchline: John's 'tasting more like meat' is a hilariously minimal endorsement — the word 'more' does all the work, implying it previously didn't taste like meat and still hasn't fully arrived.
jokes.explanation: This is a gem. 'Tasting more like meat' as a compliment is brilliant in its damning understatement. The word 'more' implies a journey from non-meat-like toward meat-like that the dish may not have completed. It's a perfect deadpan put-down disguised as encouragement. High economy, strong originality for a restaurant joke, and very John Rose — he's too polite to be honest, too honest to be polite.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So you're on board? This is great!

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No, I was just saying the plate is heavy.

jokes.text: Oh yeah, it's always quiet before the lunch rush. / Which isn't so much a rush as much as it is just a... / A handful of people showing up at a leisurely pace.
jokes.setup: John comments on the quiet diner; Twyla begins to explain the 'lunch rush.'
jokes.punchline: John finishes Twyla's sentence for her with 'a handful of people showing up at a leisurely pace' — a perfectly deflating description of small-town commerce.
jokes.explanation: Lovely fish-out-of-water moment. John completing Twyla's sentence with a more accurate (and funnier) description of Schitt's Creek commerce is well-structured. 'A leisurely pace' is the perfect word choice — gently devastating. Good collaborative rhythm between the characters.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, let's be honest, what would I do for you? I mean, what do you have that I would want? Your coupons? Your business sense? Your charm? Your hair?

jokes.text: And I thought about bringing this up the other day, but decided against it, for some reason. But here we are talking, so, what the hell.
jokes.setup: John is building to asking Twyla if he can use a diner booth as his office.
jokes.punchline: The rambling, self-aware preamble — especially 'for some reason' — captures a man who used to have assistants to handle things like this, now awkwardly negotiating on his own behalf.
jokes.explanation: More of a character observation than a joke, but 'for some reason' and 'what the hell' reveal John's discomfort navigating situations without support staff. Not sharply written but carries character resonance.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Is it safe for me to be in here? I don't want to make things weird.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, we dated for years. I think we're past the point of things being weird.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, yes, we were... we were together. In a romantic... capacity. For an extended... time period.

jokes.text: I am just gonna put these plates down. I just think I'll think better, once the blood rushes from my arms, back to my head.
jokes.setup: John has been rambling at length while Twyla holds heavy plates.
jokes.punchline: Twyla's polite, absurd explanation for needing to put the plates down — implying John has literally drained blood from her brain with his long-windedness — is a perfect Twyla non-sequitur.
jokes.explanation: Twyla's logic — that she needs blood to rush back to her brain from her arms — is medically dubious and perfectly character-specific. She says it with complete sincerity. The joke is simultaneously about John's oblivious monologuing and Twyla's gentle, weird way of handling it. Strong character integration for both.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I'm over it. I'm completely over it.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: What I meant to say is, I'm over her. I'm not over the fact that she's with someone else.

jokes.text: Happy to help, I'm sure you'd do the same for me. [beat] If you had anything to offer.
jokes.setup: Bob offers to show John office space, and John thanks him saying he'd return the favor.
jokes.punchline: John's pause, then '...if you had anything to offer' — delivered as a mumbled aside — undercuts his own politeness with devastating honesty about the power imbalance.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The beat between 'I'm sure you'd do the same for me' and 'If you had anything to offer' is perfectly timed — John's old-money condescension breaks through his diplomatic surface. The economy is near-perfect: six words that reveal everything about John's self-perception and his complicated relationship with small-town life. The pause (structural) is essential. This is the kind of line that gets quoted.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're clearly obsessed with how you look.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I am not obsessed with how I look. I just... take pride in my appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You spent forty minutes on your hair this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That's called grooming, Alexis. It's what adults do.

jokes.text: Is this a safe space yet, for us? Or...
jokes.setup: Alexis enters what appears to be Patrick's store/David's business space, tentatively.
jokes.punchline: Repurposing therapy/progressive language ('safe space') for the mundane situation of post-breakup awkwardness is typical Alexis — using trendy vocabulary without full understanding.
jokes.explanation: The 'safe space' line is a good Alexis moment — she applies wellness language to a social navigation problem. The trailing 'Or...' adds to the character's uncertainty under the breezy vocabulary. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Maybe we should take some time before we think about getting engaged again.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, yes! That's such a good idea. I think that's really smart. Yeah, let's definitely do that.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Is that a trick question?
jokes.setup: Patrick (or David's business partner) offers Alexis a 'lift' to get groceries.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's suspicion that a simple offer of a ride is a 'trick question' reveals either lingering awkwardness from their romantic history or her general paranoia about sincerity.
jokes.explanation: A clean, economical character beat. Alexis's inability to accept uncomplicated kindness after an awkward history is funny, but the joke is lightweight. Works in context.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, I... Well, you see, the thing is... I think that maybe... possibly... we should probably... I mean, not that there's anything wrong with you, because you're like, super great, and your hair is really... it's doing a thing. But I just... I can't... We can't... I'm sorry, I just need to say this and I'm going to say it but I'm also not going to say it, but I am. Ted, I think we should break up. No, wait, that came out wrong. What I meant was... I think we need to take some time. Actually, no. I think... I know that this is really hard to hear, but I have to be honest with you and with myself, and the honest truth is that I don't think this is working anymore, and I'm really sorry, and you're amazing, but I'm just not... I can't... I'm breaking up with you. I'm so sorry.

jokes.text: Yeah, I don't like you anymore. I mean, like that.
jokes.setup: The person she's with tells her she doesn't have to tiptoe around them, that they're 'over it.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'I don't like you anymore' — before the crucial clarification 'I mean, like that' — creates a moment of genuine discomfort before the repair. The pause between those two sentences is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The structural gap between 'I don't like you anymore' and 'I mean, like that' is the engine here. It's a small-scale misdirection. The clarification is almost worse — it's an awkward repair that itself needs repairing. Good cringe mechanics in compact form.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: So this is your office space.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I see. Well, it's... it's very compact.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's cozy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a closet, Bob.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: It's a storage room with potential.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: There is no potential here. There's barely room to breathe. I can't conduct business in a closet.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, where else would you like to work?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Anywhere but here would be preferable, Bob.

jokes.text: Are you wearing makeup? / So what if I am?
jokes.setup: An awkward post-breakup car ride scene.
jokes.punchline: The non sequitur observation about makeup, followed by a defensive 'so what if I am,' implies the other person has made extra effort for this casual encounter — and knows it.
jokes.explanation: A nice character beat — the defensive 'so what if I am' says everything about the emotional state of whoever it is. The 'over it' claim is immediately undermined by grooming effort. Good ironic subtext.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A desk. A chair. Perhaps a filing cabinet. Maybe some pens. A telephone would be nice. A computer of some kind. Some paper. Possibly some tape. A stapler. Some sort of organizational system. And maybe, just maybe, a window.

jokes.text: Oh my God, it's like we're sharing a brain! [then Ted reveals he wants to try the proposal again]
jokes.setup: Ted says he's been thinking about things and it didn't feel right — Alexis eagerly agrees, thinking he's breaking up with her.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's triumphant 'it's like we're sharing a brain!' is immediately followed by Ted revealing he means the OPPOSITE — he wants to try the proposal again. The cringe of her enthusiasm right before the trap closes is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured misdirection. The scene systematically misleads both Alexis and the audience — every line sounds like a breakup until the pivot. 'It's like we're sharing a brain!' is the perfect comedic apex right before the trap closes. The cringe of her jubilation makes the reveal funnier. One of the episode's structural highlights.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I couldn't break up with him because every time I tried, he'd start talking about his cases, and his voice gets really deep and authoritative, and then he smells like baby powder, and I just... I couldn't do it.

jokes.text: Alexis's face after Ted says 'we try the whole proposal thing again' — a long, horrified silence.
jokes.setup: Alexis was certain Ted was breaking up with her and had visibly celebrated too soon.
jokes.punchline: The silence after Ted's revelation — the physical performance of her realization — is the punchline to the whole misdirection sequence.
jokes.explanation: The pause/reaction beat is the payoff for the whole Ted/Alexis misdirection sequence. 'Hmm? Mmm-hmm...' and the drifting responses suggest she's completely frozen. The economy of silence is doing the work. Earned because of the elaborate setup.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just realized something. People who marry by default are basically just giving up. Like, they're settling for someone because they're afraid of being alone, or because it's easier than actually looking for love.

jokes.text: Unless you were gonna say something else? [long pause] No!
jokes.setup: Alexis is clearly not on board with Ted's proposal re-do plan but is trapped by having agreed enthusiastically moments ago.
jokes.punchline: Ted's gentle 'Unless you were gonna say something else?' gives Alexis an out she cannot take — her 'No!' is a complete lie delivered with panic, and both characters know it.
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'No!' is the entire joke — it's an eternity of Alexis considering saying the truth and choosing not to. Ted's question is either oblivious or slightly sadistic, and both readings are funny. The 'No!' being capitalized/emphatic makes it more obviously desperate. Strong cringe structure.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What happens to people who can't say no to marriage?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They get divorced.

jokes.text: 'Cause you and me... / You and I... / No, uh-huh, uh-huh, uh-huh! / It's-mmm!
jokes.setup: Ted continues enthusiastically about their relationship while Alexis appears to be physically incapable of stopping him.
jokes.punchline: The grammar correction ('You and me... / You and I') is a tiny absurdist detour in the middle of a crisis, and Alexis's sounds of forced enthusiasm ('uh-huh, uh-huh') are escalating panic dressed as agreement.
jokes.explanation: The grammar correction in the middle of Alexis's romantic catastrophe is a lovely absurdist touch. Her vocalizations ('uh-huh, uh-huh!') are pure performance-driven comedy, but the writing sets it up perfectly. The escalation of her trapped agreement is well-sustained.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You got over me pretty quickly.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I'm getting over it more as we speak.

jokes.text: What do I think? I think it's a nice garage. Where's the office? / You're looking at it.
jokes.setup: Bob promised John a 'sweet little office space.'
jokes.punchline: The 'sweet little office space' is literally a garage, and Bob's 'you're looking at it' is delivered without embarrassment.
jokes.explanation: Bob's deadpan 'you're looking at it' is perfectly delivered comedy of incompatible expectations. John's indignation vs. Bob's complete lack of self-awareness about the quality of his offer is the engine here. Not transcendent but reliably funny.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm definitely over Patrick. Like, completely over him.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Actually, I was over him the moment he left. It was instant.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: In fact, I was over him before he even left. I was already over it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I've been over him this whole time.

jokes.text: Oh, well, we got a uh, I got a great spot for a chair right there... you just have to move these tires.
jokes.setup: John complains there isn't even a chair in the so-called office.
jokes.punchline: Bob's 'great spot for a chair' — which requires moving tires — is the small-town logic of someone who genuinely sees this as a reasonable situation.
jokes.explanation: Bob's earnest problem-solving is the joke — he sees the tires as a minor obstacle rather than evidence that this is not an office. Character-driven, functional comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How long should I cook this for?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Until it's cooked, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but how long is that?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Until it's cooked.

jokes.text: Um, filing those papers'll give you some extra space. / Yeah, Bob, I'm not working for you, I'm not filing papers.
jokes.setup: Bob keeps suggesting ways John could 'improve' the garage, which all involve John doing Bob's work.
jokes.punchline: John's patience finally identifies the pattern — every 'helpful' suggestion benefits Bob, not John.
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Bob's 'helpful suggestions' are free labor extraction from John, and Bob seems only slightly aware of this. John naming it directly is the payoff. Good escalation of the Bob/John dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I folded it the way one folds cheese. It's not complicated.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you don't fold cheese. You slice it. Or you crumble it. You certainly don't fold it like it's a letter to the Queen.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, maybe if you had actually explained what you wanted instead of just standing there with that expression on your face—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This expression is my resting face, David. It's not a instruction manual.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's more of a warning label.

jokes.text: How about I give it a think without cleaning it up! / Suit yourself. As long as it gets cleaned up at some point.
jokes.setup: John refuses to clean up Bob's garage as part of the 'office arrangement.'
jokes.punchline: Bob's 'as long as it gets cleaned up at some point' — completely ignoring John's refusal and maintaining the assumption that John will eventually clean it — is perfect Bob obliviousness.
jokes.explanation: Bob's response to John's emphatic refusal is to simply push the cleaning to 'some point' — completely steamrolling John's no. The cheerful intractability is very Bob and very funny. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How do you fold cheese?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You fold it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, but how?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You just fold it.

jokes.text: I finally went over to break up with Ted. / Ooh, that is good! I was starting to think you might never do it.
jokes.setup: Alexis arrives and announces she finally broke up with Ted.
jokes.punchline: David's 'I was starting to think you might never do it' is delivered with the frankness of a sibling who has been waiting for this — no cushioning, no sympathy, just 'finally.'
jokes.explanation: David's lack of softening — 'I was starting to think you might never do it' — is a reliable sibling honesty joke. Works because of their dynamic. Not especially original but well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You folded the cheese!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I folded it to preserve its structural integrity!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You don't fold cheese, Moira!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I'm folding it again!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Don't you dare fold that cheese!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm folding it into a swan!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You fold that cheese and we are done!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We are already done, David. We've been done!

jokes.text: I don't know if it was the timbre of his voice, or the fact that he smelled like baby powder, but I just, I physically couldn't do it!
jokes.setup: Alexis reveals she did not actually break up with Ted.
jokes.punchline: Her reasons — 'the timbre of his voice' and 'he smelled like baby powder' — are so specific, so Alexis, and so unhelpful as reasons that they're both funny and revealing. The physical impossibility of breaking up ('physically couldn't') due to scent is delightfully absurd.
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Alexis — the reasons she gives for her cowardice are so specific and so narcissistically sensation-driven that they reveal everything about her character. 'Timbre of his voice' and 'baby powder' are perfect details. The phrase 'I physically couldn't do it' is both absurd and somehow emotionally true. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: If I hear the words 'fold in' one more time, I'm going to lose it.

jokes.text: You know, there must be people out there who say yes to getting married because they can't say no, and then what happens? And then they're just stuck for the rest of their life, with the same wrong, sweet, person, right?
jokes.setup: Alexis is rationalizing not having broken up with Ted.
jokes.punchline: The rhetorical spiral ends with 'the same wrong, sweet, person, right?' — a description that reveals her actual feelings better than any direct statement. She's describing herself in the third person while directly describing her situation.
jokes.explanation: The humor here is gentle and dark — Alexis is basically predicting a tragic outcome for a real population while describing her own situation. 'Wrong, sweet person' is a lovely turn of phrase. Not laugh-out-loud but clever writing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Why is it smoking?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is that supposed to happen?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: How do I turn it off?

jokes.text: Well, I'm pretty sure those people get divorced.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks what happens to people who say yes to marriage when they shouldn't.
jokes.punchline: David's answer is completely literal, delivered with no dramatic weight — 'I'm pretty sure those people get divorced' — as if she's asked a basic trivia question.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and structure. Alexis has built a tortured philosophical question and David answers it as a matter of public knowledge. 'I'm pretty sure' is doing beautiful work — it's faux-uncertainty about an obvious answer. This is David at his most effective: deflating his sister's drama with brutal pragmatism.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm going to put down the cat now.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, what? You're going to put down Mr. Whiskers?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm going to put down the cat so I can examine him.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Ted, no! You can't just put down Mr. Whiskers!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I need to set the cat down on the table to check his vitals.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ohhh... okay, that makes so much more sense.

jokes.text: What if I sent him the sweetest little text message?
jokes.setup: David tells Alexis she needs to be honest with Ted in person.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately proposes a text message as an alternative — the most cowardly possible option, described as 'sweetest little' to soften it.
jokes.explanation: The 'sweetest little text message' is a pitch-perfect Alexis line — she acknowledges the wrongness by pre-sweetening the vehicle. Deeply in character. The beat where David doesn't answer and she talks herself into doing it in person is a nice structural comedy ladder.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, Ted, you're actually really smart.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Thank you?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But...

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: There it is.

jokes.text: Okay. Yeah. / I'll do it in person. / Obviously, I'm just... / I'm gonna do it in person. / I will do this in person!
jokes.setup: David's silence after Alexis suggests a breakup text.
jokes.punchline: Alexis talks herself through the decision in real-time, the escalating proclamations growing more emphatic as her confidence decreases — 'I will do this in person!' is the most desperate because it's the least convincing.
jokes.explanation: The escalating self-promises are perfectly structured — each repetition is less convincing than the last, which is the joke. 'I will do this in person!' sounds like she's convincing herself of something she's certain she won't do. Great escalation mechanics. David's silent judgment is inferred and essential.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: There's no 'but.'

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But...

jokes.text: Well, that was quick. / It was a pretty short grocery list. / No, I mean, you being 'over it.'
jokes.setup: The other character comments it was 'quick' after Alexis returns.
jokes.punchline: Alexis deliberately misunderstands (or pretends to), claiming he means the grocery run — the correction ('No, I mean, you being over it') lands harder because she knew exactly what he meant.
jokes.explanation: A small misdirection that works because Alexis's deflection feels genuine — she might actually be that obtuse, or she might be buying time. Either reading is funny. Clean economy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I can't accept your proposal.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Yeah, I gathered that.

jokes.text: You know, it's interesting, the longer we have this conversation, the more over it I get.
jokes.setup: The other character is pressing Alexis about whether she's really over him, suggesting she might not be.
jokes.punchline: Alexis turns the pressure back on him — the very act of discussing it is actively producing her recovery, in real time, which is both a rebuttal and a joke about the nature of the conversation.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest lines. The logic is airtight and funny: 'the longer we have this conversation, the more over it I get.' It's a perfect comic inversion — his concern is producing the very thing he's questioning. 'In fact, I'm having a hard time remembering when I wasn't over it' escalates it beautifully. Both defensive and playfully aggressive.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: And I already paid for that couples' retreat in Turks and Caicos. Non-refundable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, we're breaking up.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I know, but it's non-refundable.

jokes.text: Wow, well aren't you just a... A pillar of strength, and self-healing. / Yeah, I'm pretty amazing. / Uh huh. / You really missed out. / Oh, did I? / 'Cause, you know, I'm over it. / You're over it! Yep.
jokes.setup: Alexis has declared herself over it with increasing bravado.
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic 'pillar of strength and self-healing' is meant to deflate her, but Alexis takes it completely straight — 'Yeah, I'm pretty amazing' — and escalates to 'you really missed out,' which is both absurd and somehow true.
jokes.explanation: Alexis receiving sarcasm as a compliment is pure character gold. 'Yeah, I'm pretty amazing' followed by 'you really missed out' shows how her narcissism doubles as armor. The exchange ends in a kind of exhausted draw that's more charming than mean. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm looking at manga.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I thought I might take it up as a career.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you're fifty-five.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, the perfect age to start drawing Japanese cartoons.

jokes.text: Yeah, I'm pretty amazing. — You really missed out.
jokes.setup: Patrick sarcastically compliments David's emotional resilience.
jokes.punchline: David entirely misses (or ignores) the sarcasm and accepts the compliment at face value, adding 'You really missed out' — which is somehow both a joke and slightly true.
jokes.explanation: David receiving sarcasm as sincere praise is deeply character-specific — his narcissism isn't cruel, it's almost innocent. 'You really missed out' is a perfectly delivered callback to the romantic history with exactly the right amount of ambiguity about whether he's joking.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, you know, Johnny, I hate to break it to you, but you're broke. You have no money. And frankly, the fact that you're still married is a miracle.

jokes.text: Fold in the cheese / What does that mean? / What does 'fold in the cheese' mean? / You fold it in. / I understand that, but how?
jokes.setup: Moira gives cooking instructions: 'fold in the cheese.'
jokes.punchline: David's question 'what does fold in the cheese MEAN?' launches the funniest sequence in the episode — the instruction makes perfect sense to Moira and zero sense to David, and neither can bridge the gap.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic centerpiece and one of Schitt's Creek's most celebrated moments. The setup is perfect: Moira gives an instruction that is technically clear but functionally useless to someone who has never cooked. David's question — 'How do you fold it? Do you fold it in half, like a piece of paper, and drop it in the pot?' — is both absurd and completely logical for someone without cooking knowledge. The escalating inability of Moira to explain something she cannot demonstrate is the engine, and it runs beautifully. This is exceptional character integration: only Moira would be this imperious about her own inability to explain, and only David would ask this question with this much specificity and indignation. Cultural touchstone level comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Okay, so you're gonna be here at 6 AM to open up. You'll need to unlock the front gate, flip the sign, turn on all the lights, and get the coffee going.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: 6 AM? I thought we were just sharing the space.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, now you're sharing it as an employee. And employees open.

jokes.text: David, I cannot show you everything. / Okay, well, can you show me one thing?!
jokes.setup: Moira refuses to demonstrate 'folding in the cheese,' retreating behind imperiousness.
jokes.punchline: David's 'can you show me ONE thing?!' exposes that Moira has shown him nothing — her response to his request for help is to insist he should already know things she's never taught him.
jokes.explanation: This escalation within the folding sequence is exceptional. 'I cannot show you everything' is Moira at her most grandly useless — the implication that she HAS shown him things, when she manifestly hasn't, is the joke. 'Can you show me ONE thing?!' is both a devastating indictment and a perfectly timed escalation. The mother-son dynamic crystallizes perfectly here.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was once very practical. I could change a tire, make a soufflé, and I knew the difference between a bidet and a... well, I still don't know the difference between a bidet and a douche.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But that was before the money. Now I can't do any of those things. I can't even open a jar without calling someone.

jokes.text: How do you fold it? Do you fold it in half, like a piece of paper, and drop it in the pot, or, what do you do?
jokes.setup: David needs to understand 'folding in' cheese.
jokes.punchline: His specific, wrong interpretation — folding cheese 'like a piece of paper' and dropping it in — is absurdly literal and reveals the complete absence of any culinary instinct.
jokes.explanation: The paper-folding interpretation is the single most quotable moment in the sequence. It's specific enough to be perfectly absurd and logical enough to make sense to someone who has never cooked. 'Drop it in the pot' as a follow-up image is inspired. This is the line people quote.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm not like you and your sister. I have a certain je ne sais quoi.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, that's not really—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's French, David. I wouldn't expect you to understand.

jokes.text: You just... here's what you do: You just fold it in.
jokes.setup: Moira finally agrees to show David how to fold in the cheese.
jokes.punchline: Her entire 'demonstration' is just saying 'you just fold it in' with no additional information — proving she has no idea how to explain it either.
jokes.explanation: This is the escalation peak of the 'fold in the cheese' bit. Moira's 'demonstration' is identical to her instruction — she cannot show him anything more because she doesn't know either. This retroactively reframes everything: Moira doesn't know how to fold in the cheese. Her authority over the recipe is a performance. The joke about the Rose family's collective incompetence crystallizes perfectly. Highest craft score in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I know exactly how it feels to be utterly helpless. I have three children.

jokes.text: You just... here's what you do: You just fold it in. / If you say 'fold in' one more time... / It says, fold it in! / This is your recipe! / You fold in the cheese, then! / Don't you dare! / You fold it in! / David!
jokes.setup: Moira tries to demonstrate 'folding in' and can only repeat the phrase.
jokes.punchline: The escalating loop — Moira unable to say anything but 'fold it in,' David threatening consequences, Moira continuing anyway — becomes a comedic shouting match about a cooking technique.
jokes.explanation: The apex of the folding sequence. The comedy here is structural perfection: Moira literally cannot say anything other than the phrase David has forbidden her to say, so she keeps saying it. 'If you say fold in one more time...' followed immediately by 'It says fold it in!' is a beautiful breaking of a self-imposed rule. The argument escalates to near-physical altercation over cheese folding. One of the show's most celebrated scenes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this looks absolutely simple. I could do this in my sleep.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you do it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Oh good, now I see bubbles. / David! What does burning smell like?!
jokes.setup: The folding argument has apparently distracted them from the actual cooking.
jokes.punchline: The argument ends with the sauce burning — revealed by David's oblivious 'I see bubbles' and Moira's panicked 'What does burning smell like?!' — the latter being the funniest possible question from someone who claimed to know this recipe.
jokes.explanation: 'What does burning smell like?!' is the perfect button on the cooking catastrophe. It's simultaneously absurd (anyone who has cooked knows what burning smells like) and character-specific (Moira has never cooked enough to know intuitively). It's also a devastating self-own — this is her 'mother's recipe' and she can't identify burning. One of the episode's most quotable lines.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: She was incredibly intelligent, and funny, and beautiful, and deeply selfish.

jokes.text: Well, I think it's probably better if you put Hazel down. / What?! No, she'll be fine! It's just her blood sugar!
jokes.setup: Alexis arrives at the vet clinic and says she thinks 'it would be better if you put Hazel down.'
jokes.punchline: Ted immediately (and reasonably) thinks she means euthanize the animal; Alexis meant set her down physically for the conversation. The double meaning creates a moment of horror before resolution.
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection using veterinary terminology. 'Put Hazel down' has an obvious dark meaning in a vet's office, so Ted's panic ('No, she'll be fine!') is completely warranted. The clarification adds a layer of Alexis-ian obliviousness — she didn't even register the double meaning. Well-constructed with good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They're... they're very... they're something.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I mean, you can really taste the... effort.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And that's... that's what's important.

jokes.text: Well, that was pretty direct. / People like you deserve better than people like me.
jokes.setup: Alexis says 'People who deserve more directness. People like you.'
jokes.punchline: Ted responds to what he thinks is a compliment ('that was pretty direct') only for Alexis to immediately follow with 'people like you deserve better than people like me' — a self-deprecating bombshell that reframes the directness as bad news.
jokes.explanation: This is the kind of cringe comedy where the laugh comes from the structure — Ted thinks he's receiving a compliment and is blindsided. The rhythm of 'well, that was pretty direct' followed by the actual content being devastating is well-timed. Alexis's self-awareness here is also slightly out of character in a way that reads as genuine growth, which adds warmth.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's like eating a tire fire, but like, in a good way.

jokes.text: You're the perfect boyfriend, Ted. / Thank you... / But... / I knew there was a 'but.' / No, no, no! There is no 'but.' / But you're just not the perfect boyfriend for me.
jokes.setup: Alexis says 'You're the perfect boyfriend' then Ted braces for the 'but.'
jokes.punchline: When Alexis says 'No, no, no! There is no but' and then immediately produces a 'but' — it's a double misdirection. Ted was right, Alexis was wrong about being wrong.
jokes.explanation: The 'I knew there was a but / there is no but / but...' exchange is a neat structural trick. The brief denial of the 'but' before its immediate arrival makes both the denial and the but funnier. Ted's resigned 'I knew there was a but' is also perfectly delivered. Good construction.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We could cook regularly. It's not that hard. I mean, how difficult can it be to just... fold in the cheese?

jokes.text: Yeah, no, I pieced that part together.
jokes.setup: Alexis, after the whole breakup speech, clarifies she can't accept his proposal.
jokes.punchline: Ted's 'I pieced that part together' is drily funny — he didn't need the explicit clarification that refusing to continue the relationship = declining the proposal.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. 'Yeah, no, I pieced that part together' says everything — it's a gentle, dry acknowledgment that Alexis is over-explaining. The 'Yeah, no' construction is particularly effective as a softening preface to a mild put-down. Ted's humor in heartbreak is a good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was telling David about my morning, and he looked at me like I had three heads.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because you were describing a conversation you had with Alexis, but you kept saying 'you.'

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, how was I supposed to know? You were both standing there!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We were not both standing there. I was in the back room for twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A detail I was unaware of at the time.

jokes.text: Oh wow, could you make this any harder? / Well, I did book us a two week couples vacation that's non-refundable.
jokes.setup: Ted is being extraordinarily gracious about the breakup, which Alexis finds guilt-inducing.
jokes.punchline: After Alexis says 'could you make this any harder,' Ted reveals he's booked a non-refundable couples vacation — which makes it objectively harder.
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup/punchline construction. 'Could you make this any harder?' is a rhetorical question that Ted answers literally and specifically. The 'non-refundable' detail is the perfect escalation — it adds a practical consequence to the emotional devastation. Alexis's 'Ooh, yeah, that makes... that makes it harder' is a well-earned callback button. Strong impact.

jokes.text: It's funny to hear that, because before you were saying this isn't office space. / And uh, well, I got to thinking, well, if it's not office space, then, what the heck is it?!
jokes.setup: John returns to the garage to claim the office space after previously rejecting it.
jokes.punchline: Bob's question 'if it's not office space, then what the heck is it?!' is a completely unanswerable question about his own garage — the implied answer (a garage) is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Bob's philosophical crisis about the identity of his garage is funny in its way — he seems to have genuinely reconsidered the space's purpose. His earnestness makes the absurdism work. Good Bob character moment.

jokes.text: They're Japanese graphic novels. It's called Manga. / Oh well, that sounds like a... Lucrative career choice.
jokes.setup: Bob describes his nephew Eric as drawing 'Chinese cartoons.'
jokes.punchline: Eric corrects this ('Japanese graphic novels, it's called Manga') and John's response — 'that sounds like a... lucrative career choice' — is barely-disguised sarcasm with a pregnant pause before 'lucrative.'
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'lucrative' is everything — it signals John's true opinion while maintaining surface-level politeness. The setup (Bob's casual cultural confusion about 'Chinese cartoons') is also funny in its own right. A two-layer joke with good economy.

jokes.text: Either does Johnny, and he's not even related to me. / No, but you said Eric was Gwen's nephew, so technically he's not related to you, either. So... no offense, Eric. / No, that's fair.
jokes.setup: Bob says Eric (who has no money) can't have the office, same as Johnny.
jokes.punchline: John correctly points out that Eric isn't actually Bob's blood relative — Bob's own logic is inconsistent — and Eric immediately agrees ('that's fair'), making it a three-way comedy moment.
jokes.explanation: The best Bob/John garage moment. John's logical dismantling of Bob's family-loyalty argument is satisfying, and Eric's 'no, that's fair' is an unexpected and delightful third beat. Eric agreeing to his own dismissal is absurdist comedy gold — he has no stake in arguing. The 'no offense, Eric' before Eric even responds is also funny.

jokes.text: Well, that's okay, Uncle Bob. I don't have any money. — Either does Johnny, and he's not even related to me.
jokes.setup: Eric defers because he has no money; Bob uses this to settle the dispute.
jokes.punchline: Bob notes that Johnny also has no money AND isn't even related to him — pointing out that Eric has a stronger claim on Bob's charity than Johnny does.
jokes.explanation: A very well-constructed logical point delivered with deadpan precision. Bob's observation that Johnny has no money and no familial claim is technically correct and completely undermines Johnny's position. 'He's not even related to me' lands as both funny and slightly devastating for Johnny.

jokes.text: Bob, I don't work for you, I'm just setting up an office! / No, I know, it's your space. And uh, just so you know, garage opens at eight, but if you're here a few minutes before that, even better!
jokes.setup: John once again insists he doesn't work for Bob.
jokes.punchline: Bob verbally concedes ('it's your space') while immediately treating John like an employee with a start time.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the entire garage subplot. Bob's 'I know, it's your space' immediately followed by 'the garage opens at eight, but if you're here a few minutes before that, even better' is a masterclass in oblivious manipulation. He never actually acknowledges John as anything other than an employee. Great callback/running gag conclusion.

jokes.text: You smell the room, what do you think? / I-I don't know. / Well, of course you don't, because I have not taught you life's basic skills.
jokes.setup: David and Moira survey the aftermath of their cooking disaster.
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots from the burning disaster to a broader indictment of her parenting — 'I have not taught you life's basic skills' — taking both credit and blame simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Moira's self-centered reframe of the cooking disaster as a parenting failure (which is simultaneously an admission AND a deflection) is very Moira. 'I have not taught you life's basic skills' is delivered as if this is a recent discovery rather than lifelong neglect. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Other than cooking, what basic life skills don't I have? / Oh David, I'm not sure we have time for that.
jokes.setup: David pushes back on Moira's claim that he lacks basic life skills.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'I'm not sure we have time for that' implies the list is so long it would be impractical to enumerate — a devastating maternal put-down delivered as time management.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke. 'I'm not sure we have time for that' as a response to 'what basic skills don't I have?' is exceptional in its economy and cruelty-disguised-as-practicality. The implication — that the list would take so long it isn't worth starting — is the most devastating possible answer. Pure Moira. This is the caliber of joke that represents the show at its best.

jokes.text: I could make a bed, I could fix a wobbly table, or start a car without a key.
jokes.setup: Moira is listing her former competencies from her pre-wealth life.
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from mundane domestic skills to 'start a car without a key' — which is either hotwiring (crime) or something equally incongruous, and she lists it with complete naturalness.
jokes.explanation: 'Start a car without a key' is the unexpected item in the enumeration — escalating from 'make a bed' and 'fix a wobbly table' to what is essentially hotwiring a vehicle. Moira says it without any acknowledgment that it's different in kind from the other skills. Perfect delivery moment; strong character integration (Moira's murky past).

jokes.text: Then I met your father, and suddenly I had people to do everything for me. And I assumed that I would maintain my proficiencies, should I ever, God forbid, need them. But no, I have lost all my skills.
jokes.setup: Moira reflects on how marriage to a wealthy man ended her self-reliance.
jokes.punchline: 'God forbid' for the prospect of needing her own skills — she genuinely hoped to never use them again. The realization that she assumed she'd retain skills she never practiced is also funny.
jokes.explanation: The 'God forbid I ever need them' is a lovely dark touch — she genuinely prayed she'd never need self-sufficiency. The line about assuming she'd 'maintain her proficiencies' without practicing them is also very Moira: grandiose self-deception. Warm and funny, with character depth.

jokes.text: And now I know how it feels to be utterly helpless, like you, and your sister. / I don't know if I'd call us 'helpless.' / No need to make me feel better.
jokes.setup: Moira concludes that she now understands helplessness — by comparing herself to David and Alexis.
jokes.punchline: David mildly objects; Moira's 'no need to make me feel better' interprets his objection as COMFORT rather than pushback — she's so committed to her own pathos she can't see his actual point.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest jokes. Moira comparing David and Alexis to herself as the pinnacle of helplessness — as an insult that doesn't register as an insult — and then interpreting David's pushback as emotional support for HER is character comedy of the highest order. 'No need to make me feel better' is simultaneously oblivious and devastating. It works on multiple levels: as Moira's self-centeredness, as a genuine maternal insult, and as absurdist logic. Very high character integration for both.

jokes.text: David shows Moira a YouTube tutorial on 'folding' — 'Folding is one of the easiest techniques in the book. It's just a very gentle way of combining two ingredients.' / Well, that's simple enough, any fool could do that. / Did you do that?! / No.
jokes.setup: David has found a video explaining the folding technique that Moira couldn't teach.
jokes.punchline: Moira declares 'any fool could do that' — after their entire catastrophic exchange about not knowing how — and then David's 'Did you do that?!' / 'No.' is the perfect landing.
jokes.explanation: This is the perfect callback button on the episode's best joke sequence. 'Any fool could do that' — from the woman who just spent five minutes unable to explain or demonstrate it — is devastating. And then the three-word exchange: 'Did you do that?! / No.' is one of the tightest comedy exchanges in the episode. The 'No' deserves its own laugh track. Near-exceptional comedy writing: Moira's self-awareness gaps are endless and the callback structure is immaculate.

jokes.text: She really made a mark on me, you know? And it's rare to find someone that beautiful, and smart, and deeply selfish, yet charming!
jokes.setup: Ted is pouring out his heart to John about Alexis after the breakup.
jokes.punchline: 'Deeply selfish, yet charming!' — Ted lists Alexis's flaw in the middle of her virtues with the same tone as the compliments, as if selfishness is just another appealing trait.
jokes.explanation: This is a wonderful joke because it's also completely accurate character description. 'Beautiful, smart, and deeply selfish, yet charming' is a pitch-perfect summary of Alexis that only someone who genuinely loves her could deliver without cruelty. Ted saying it in the same tone as 'beautiful and smart' is the joke — he's not complaining, he's admiring. High originality, excellent character integration for both Ted (earnest, loving) and Alexis (accurately described in absentia).

jokes.text: Well, this looks delicious! I see someone decided on a new dish. / Nope, these are the enchiladas.
jokes.setup: John sees the food and assumes it's something other than enchiladas given the cooking disaster.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Nope, these are the enchiladas' — delivered flatly, presumably over a dish that looks nothing like enchiladas — is the underplayed landing of the entire cooking subplot.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback button. The fact that the dish looks enough unlike enchiladas that John thinks it's 'a new dish' tells you everything about how the cooking went. Moira's flat denial — 'Nope, these are the enchiladas' — is perfectly committed. Good economy.

jokes.text: Yeah well, they kinda smell like enchiladas, so...
jokes.setup: John says the dish looks like it might be something other than enchiladas.
jokes.punchline: Roland's contribution — 'they kinda smell like enchiladas' — sets the bar at 'vaguely smells like the claimed dish,' which is the lowest possible positive endorsement.
jokes.explanation: Roland's endorsement of 'they kinda smell like enchiladas, so...' is a perfect Roland line — low bar, genuine goodwill, somehow comforting. The callback to the burning-smell panic makes the 'kinda smell like' even funnier. 'So...' trailing off implies this is sufficient for him.

jokes.text: We had lots of fun making them. / Mm! Let's not ruin a meal by talking about the process!
jokes.setup: Moira says they had fun; David's reaction presumably suggests otherwise.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Let's not ruin a meal by talking about the process!' is both defensive and imperious — she's burying the disaster under table etiquette.
jokes.explanation: 'Let's not ruin a meal by talking about the process!' is a beautifully quotable Moira line. It acknowledges the catastrophe implicitly while refusing to discuss it — managing the narrative with imperious hospitality. The economy is excellent, the character integration is near-perfect (only Moira would use etiquette as a cover-up), and the callback value is high.

jokes.text: The family eating the enchiladas in near-silence, then: 'Not bad. Not bad, you two! Not bad at all!'
jokes.setup: After the cooking disaster sequence, the enchiladas are served. Everyone eats in loaded silence.
jokes.punchline: The long pause of eating followed by the minimal 'not bad' — not a rave review, just relief — is the perfect understatement payoff for the cooking disaster.
jokes.explanation: The understated payoff to the cooking arc. 'Not bad' after all that chaos is appropriately humble. The visual beat of everyone tentatively eating before speaking is the real joke — the silence earns the line.

jokes.text: Although it's really not that complicated, once you fold in the cheese! / Ahh...
jokes.setup: After dinner, the family discusses possibly doing this again; Moira casually mentions the folding technique.
jokes.punchline: Moira saying 'once you fold in the cheese' — after the entire catastrophic sequence — and David's pained 'Ahh...' is the final callback button on the episode's centerpiece joke.
jokes.explanation: The perfect final callback. Moira says 'fold in the cheese' one more time, with complete ownership — as if she has always known how to do this and always been able to explain it. David's 'Ahh...' is the final receipt for the entire sequence. The economy of this callback is extraordinary: four words do all the work of referencing everything that came before. The audience's collective 'I remember!' reaction is the engine. This is exactly how callbacks should work — they should feel both surprising and inevitable.

jokes.text: Well, this could be a very good thing! I think maybe once, or twice a week... — This won't be happening again. — Uh-uh.
jokes.setup: The family enjoys the enchiladas; Johnny suggests they could cook together regularly.
jokes.punchline: David and Moira simultaneously and emphatically shut it down: 'This won't be happening again. Uh-uh.' — a unified front against domesticity.
jokes.explanation: David and Moira's synchronized rejection of domestic normalcy despite its success is a perfect character button. The unity of 'This won't be happening again. Uh-uh.' from two people who just proved they could barely manage it once is very funny. Moira's 'Uh-uh' adding a punctuation beat is particularly good.

jokes.text: Alexis hasn't been here for a week and a half. / I had breakfast with her just yesterday. / That was me. / We had a lengthy conversation about hosiery and menopause. / Again, that was me.
jokes.setup: Someone says Alexis hasn't been around; Moira insists she's seen Alexis recently.
jokes.punchline: John clarifies that both interactions were with him — including a 'lengthy conversation about hosiery and menopause,' which he apparently engaged in fully. The escalation from 'breakfast' to 'hosiery and menopause' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The episode's closing joke and one of its best. The structure is perfect: three beats, each one more devastating. Beat 1: 'I had breakfast with her yesterday.' Beat 2: 'That was me.' Beat 3: 'We had a lengthy conversation about hosiery and menopause.' Beat 4: 'Again, that was me.' The revelation that John had a lengthy conversation about hosiery and menopause — presumably at length, in full, without mentioning he wasn't Alexis — is the absurdist peak. It also implies something deeply funny about Moira's perception of her daughter. Near-exceptional character integration and structure. The episode's second-best joke.

tmdb_overview: Moira decides to cook dinner with the help of David. Johnny scopes out an office for his consulting business. And Alexis finally comes clean with Ted about their engagement.
insight: Escalation-driven character comedy sustains 86.5 score across 57 jokes in 23 minutes.
